one_line_takeaway: A broad, taxonomy-first survey mapping LLM security attacks (jailbreaking, prompt injection, backdoor, data poisoning) and privacy attacks (gradient leakage, membership inference, PII leakage) to their defenses and open gaps, whose central message is that most known attacks are still only validated on small models, not production-scale LLMs.
key_finding: A recurring, load-bearing finding the survey extracts across categories is an EVALUATION GAP: most representative attacks and defenses were validated on small language models or small-scale LLMs (e.g., backdoor threat models on 1.3B-6B-parameter LMs; BadAgent on 6B/13B models; gradient-leakage attacks like TAG/LAMP/GBDA on Transformer LMs; PII/memorization attacks on GPT-2), so their effectiveness against large-scale LLMs such as GPT-3 (175B) remains unverified. It also stresses that security and privacy attack categories share goals (backdoor and poisoning both aim at malfunction; prompt injection and jailbreaking both aim at eliciting withheld/sensitive content), and that many privacy attacks rest on arguably unrealistic assumptions (white-box gradient access, or the adversary knowing the training-data distribution).
core_contribution: A 51-page systematization-of-knowledge survey (Das, Amini, Wu; FIU, arXiv:2402.00888v2, targeted at J. ACM) that organizes the security and privacy attack surface of Large Language Models under a single goal-based taxonomy. The paper's OWN contribution is the taxonomy and synthesis, not any new attack or defense: it splits LLM threats into SECURITY attacks (Prompt Hacking -> Jailbreaking, Prompt Injection; Adversarial Attacks -> Backdoor, Data Poisoning) and PRIVACY attacks (Gradient Leakage, Membership Inference, PII Leakage), then for each category compiles representative methods, per-attack characteristic/limitation/mitigation tables (Tables 3 and 4), a defense-mapping figure, and a research-gap/future-direction agenda. Its distinguishing claim over prior surveys is captured in its own Table 2, which scores 22 prior surveys/empirical works across 11 threat/coverage dimensions and positions this survey as giving 'extensive discussion' (three-star) on all of them.
threat_model: The survey does not defend one system; it frames a taxonomy of attacker models across the LLM lifecycle. Two canonical scenarios are illustrated: (1) a training/fine-tuning-phase security attack where a malicious developer implants a backdoor via poisoned data so a trigger token (the paper's running example is 'CF') makes a deployed model emit harmful content that bypasses safety alignment; and (2) an inference-phase privacy attack where a malicious end user crafts jailbreaking/injection prompts against a black-box deployed model to extract PII (email, phone) or reconstruct private training data. Attack entities enumerated: end users, developers, training/fine-tuning data, and the deployed model. Capabilities range from black-box query access (PII leakage, membership inference on APIs) to white-box gradient access (gradient-leakage attacks in federated-learning settings).
limitations: As a survey it produces no primary measurements, so all quantitative claims are compiled from cited works rather than independently reproduced. The Impact ratings (Low/Moderate/High/Very High) in Tables 3-4 are qualitative author judgments, not measured. Coverage is a snapshot of literature through roughly late 2024 (v2 dated Nov 2024), citing works up to April-Sep 2024. The self-comparison in Table 2 uses the authors' own three-tier discussion-depth scoring, which is inherently self-serving (their row is three-star on every dimension). Multimodal/vision-language threats are touched only lightly, and the taxonomy's placement of jailbreaking is inconsistent (labeled a privacy-attack example in one figure caption while categorized under security prompt-hacking elsewhere).

datasets_used: The survey runs no experiments; the following datasets/benchmarks appear only in the primary works it reviews, not in any measurement by the authors:
LAMA benchmark (factual-knowledge probing, used to evaluate AutoPrompt)
clinical/masked-clinical language-model corpora (referenced for MIA on MLMs)
quantitative_results: Table 2 compares this survey against 22 prior surveys/empirical works across 11 dimensions (JA, PI, BA, DPa, GLa, MIA, PII-Leak, AR, one-to-one Defense-Against-Attacks, Analysis/Future-Research-Direction, Publication Date).
Compiled from cited work TAG (Deng et al.): reconstructs up to 88.9% of tokens with 0.93 cosine similarity in token embeddings to the private training data.
Compiled from cited work LAMP (Balunovic et al.): reconstructs 5x more bi-grams and ~23% longer subsequences than TAG, and is first to recover input from batch sizes greater than 1.
Compiled from cited EasyJailbreak framework: 11 jailbreaking methods across 10 LLMs, with an average 60% violation rate under jailbreaking attacks.
Compiled from cited JailbreakEval: evaluates ~90 jailbreaking attacks proposed between May 2023 and April 2024.

Compiled from cited Shadowcast (VLM poisoning): effective with as few as 50 poisoned samples; NightShade (text-to-image poisoning): controls a prompt's output with as few as 100 poisoned training instances.

Compiled from cited CBA (composite backdoor attack): high attack success rate on Llama-7B, Llama-13B, and Llama-30B; in-context-learning backdoor threat model evaluated on LMs from 1.3B to 6B parameters.
Survey notes many attacks were validated only up to 6B/13B parameters, leaving GPT-3-scale (175B) effectiveness unexplored (author-stated gap, not a measured result).
baselines_compared: This is a survey; 'baselines' are the 22 prior surveys/empirical studies it benchmarks itself against in Table 2, including Neel et al. [182], Wu et al. [287], Gupta et al. [83], Liu et al. [147], Deng et al. [54], Yang et al. [300], Shi et al. [235], Wan et al. [263], Xin et al. [294], Jieren et al. [55], Lukas et al. [152], Carlini et al. [23], Robey et al. (SmoothLLM) [215], Vassilev et al. [258], Sun et al. [248], Zhao et al. [323], Minaee et al. [171], Naveed et al. [181], Shayegani et al. [232], Wang et al. [267], and Yao et al. [306].
